next_steps: Search Robson and Madden correspondence, Camden Society papers, and library reader records for explicit consultation evidence.
Until direct access is proved, retain Madden 1839 as the printed intermediary and do not add Robson-to-Lincoln or Robson-to-Douce manuscript edges.
openQuestions.question: Did Robson directly consult Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 or Bodleian MS. Douce 324, or did all non-Taylor Anturs readings reach him through Madden's 1839 edition and assistance?

next_steps: Request a complete foliation-aware surrogate or consult the manuscript onsite.
Determine whether the difference reflects text extent, later matter, foliation, or catalog inference before normalizing a single range.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV locate Awntyrs in MS. Douce 324 at ff. 1r–11v while the current Bodleian catalog infers ff. 1r–14v?

next_steps: Consult recent codicological and dialect studies of Taylor MS. 9 before normalizing a narrower production place.
Keep witness production and dialect evidence separate from composition places for the three romances.
openQuestions.question: How should Princeton's current catalog place/date statement, the manuscript's Merseyside ownership and production history, and the Taylor romance witnesses' LALME dialect evidence be reconciled?

next_steps: Request complete present-object imaging of Lincoln MS 91 through the cathedral's researcher service and map Awntyrs ff. 154r–161r after delivery.
Request complete MS. Douce 324 imaging through Bodleian Imaging Services and confirm the exact Awntyrs locus.
Retain Lincoln's public f. 80r Morte Arthure image as zero-overlap partial coverage and Bodleian's fols. 1r/4v selection as partial noncontinuous coverage.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, foliation-stable facsimiles of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 and Bodleian MS. Douce 324 be obtained for direct comparison?

Complete image coverage means the complete provider-published canvas inventory, not every physical blank leaf, edge, spine, or preservation-master exposure.

Standalone Princeton and NLS Canvas IDs returned HTTP 404 on 2026-07-12; exact verified Image API URLs are therefore used for human-facing work-portion boundaries, while canvas IDs remain inventory locators.

lineage.summary: CME00113 is the repository XML representation of the title matter and printed pp. 1–93 of an AMS Press 1968 reprint of John Robson's 1842 Camden Society edition of three independent Middle English romances. Robson's principal manuscript was Princeton Taylor MS. 9. He mediated selected Anturs readings through Frederick Madden's 1839 edition and its Lincoln/Douce collation, and supplied Sir Amadace stanza XXXII from Henry Weber's 1810 edition of the NLS witness. Complete provider-published image inventories are cached for Taylor MS. 9 and NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1; the Bodleian public object has only two Douce 324 images, and no complete public Lincoln MS 91 or Douce 324 facsimile was found.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous; no shared or distinct authorship is established for the three romances
catalogSummary.date: Works composed from the late fourteenth to early fifteenth century; collection first published 1842
catalogSummary.region: Northern and northwestern England; the proposed origin varies by romance
catalogSummary.title: Three Early English Metrical Romances: With an Introduction and Glossary

catalogingSubject.label: Three Early English Metrical Romances: With an Introduction and Glossary
catalogingSubject.scope_note: John Robson's 1842 selected edition of three independent anonymous Middle English romances. CME00113 represents the edition title matter and the three poem texts on printed pp. 1–93, not Robson's introduction, manuscript description, post-text notes, glossary, corrigenda, or the physical manuscripts themselves.

contentParts.label: The Awntyrs off Arthure at the Terne Wathelyne
notes: The XML has 714 direct verse lines because stanza IV has twelve lines; DIMEV describes the work's full or ideal extent as 715 lines.
Ten nested apparatus quotation lines are excluded from the direct poem-line count.
contentParts.summary: An Arthurian alliterative romance in fifty-five thirteen-line stanza slots, combining a ghostly warning about mortality and land-taking with Gawain's later combat against Galeron.

contentParts.label: Sir Amadace
notes: The XML has seventy-one LG containers but seventy-two displayed stanzas: LG N=41 contains twenty-four lines corresponding to stanzas XLI–XLII, and the following numbering resumes irregularly. These are Robson's renumbered surviving or supplied stanzas, not the full work's ideal stanza extent.
Robson states that the first four twelve-line stanzas, forty-eight lines, were lost with the leaf before surviving f. 17r; the XML's 864 lines therefore describe an acephalous text.
Robson supplied stanza XXXII from Weber's 1810 edition rather than the damaged Taylor copy.
contentParts.summary: An acephalous late Middle English romance about charity, debt, a grateful-dead compact, restored wealth, marriage, and the testing of loyalty. Robson's text begins mid-narrative because the Taylor manuscript lost the leaf containing the first forty-eight lines.

contentParts.label: The Avowynge of King Arther
notes: The XML has 1,148 direct lines because stanza XVIII has twelve rather than sixteen lines; DIMEV's full-form description implies 1,152 lines.
contentParts.summary: An Arthurian tail-rhyme romance organized around hunting, vows, hospitality, combat, and the loyal testing of Arthur, Gawain, Kay, and Baldwin.

scope.description: First publication of Robson's three-romance collection

extent.description: Repository XML representation metrics for the three poem divisions: 198 LG containers and 2,726 direct poem L children. There are 199 displayed stanzas because one Sir Amadace LG contains two numbered twelve-line stanzas. Sir Amadace's 864 lines are the acephalous Robson/Taylor text—forty-eight opening lines were lost before surviving f. 17r—not a complete-work extent. The raw descendant L count is 2,736 because ten Anturs apparatus quotations are nested as lines; it is not the poem-length total.

formStatements.description: All three cataloged works are stanzaic verse; prose paratext and apparatus belong to the edition layer.

scope.description: The language of the three medieval romances
Robson's titles, introduction, manuscript description, notes, glossary, and editorial apparatus; CME00113 omits most prose paratext

notes: CME00113 reproduces the original 1842 title page inside an AMS Press reprint title page; the immediate U-M source layer is therefore the 1968 reprint, not an original 1842 impression.
The Taylor manuscript's dialect, date, and production place are witness properties and are not silently promoted to composition claims for the three works.
Direct verse-line counts exclude nested apparatus quotations and preserve genuine incomplete stanzas rather than normalizing to ideal stanza forms.

next_steps: Apply a corpus-wide policy for extracting independently titled works from edited collections.
If separate records are created, retain this collection record for edition-level provenance and avoid duplicating the source-layer claims.
openQuestions.question: Should the three romances receive separate top-level descriptive records in addition to this edition-level collection record?

notes: A possible Carlisle association remains conjectural and is not derived from the Taylor witness's later dialect.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous poet of The Awntyrs off Arthure

scope.description: The underlying romance, distinct from Robson's edited text and its manuscript witnesses

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous poet of The Avowynge of King Arther

scope.description: Selection and arrangement of the three independent romances in one volume
Robson's base transcription of the Taylor manuscript, with separately sourced supplied passages recorded in the lineage manifest
Use of Frederick Madden's 1839 printed Lincoln text and Douce apparatus, not demonstrated direct consultation of those manuscripts

subjects.label: Almsgiving and care for the poor

summaries.value: John Robson's 1842 scholarly collection edits three independent anonymous Middle English verse romances from a principal Taylor MS. 9 text, supplemented in selected passages through Madden's and Weber's earlier printed editions. CME00113 encodes the reprint title matter and the three poem texts, not the full editorial volume.

The collection contains The Awntyrs off Arthure, Sir Amadace, and The Avowynge of King Arther. The two Arthurian works concern Gawain and Arthur's court; Sir Amadace follows a ruined knight whose charity and supernatural compact restore his fortunes.

The edition-level collection is the cataloging subject. Its three DIV2 units are independent works, not sections of one medieval composition; Taylor MS. 9 is their principal witness in Robson's edition, while Madden and Weber mediate selected supplied readings from other witnesses.

titles.value: Three Early English Metrical Romances: With an Introduction and Glossary
The Awntyrs off Arthure at the Terne Wathelyne
The Anturs of Arther at the Tarnewathelan
The Avowynge of King Arther, Sir Gawan, Sir Kaye, and Sir Bawdewyn of Bretan

work.author: Anonymous; no shared or distinct authorship is established for the three romances
work.dateDisplay: Works composed from the late fourteenth to early fifteenth century; collection first published 1842
